# PBIR format for Power BI reports — Explanation, comparison, creation, compatibility, and recovery

## 1) Executive summary

The Power BI Enhanced Report format (PBIR) is a JSON-first metadata layout introduced by Microsoft as a preview feature (announced June 2024) to store report metadata for Power BI Projects (.pbip) in a modular, source-control-friendly way. When enabled in Power BI Desktop Developer Mode, PBIR writes report metadata into a `definition/` folder as schema-declared JSON files (pages, visuals, bookmarks, themes, etc.), enabling better diffs, merge resolution, and editor tooling (IntelliSense/validation) for report metadata. Microsoft describes PBIR as the "Power BI enhanced report format" and documents the behaviour in the Power BI team blog announcement (June 2024): https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/ (accessed 2026-04-24).

PBIR is an official Microsoft metadata format for Power BI Projects but is not a wholesale replacement of PBIX/PBIT/RDL. PBIR is tied to PBIP project workflows: the PBIP remains the project container, while PBIR defines how report metadata is stored inside that container. The preview and documentation emphasize backup before upgrading and warn that manual JSON edits are brittle; they recommend PBIR-aware tools/CLI and source-control workflows to reduce risk.

---

## 2) Key findings

- Microsoft officially introduced PBIR as the "Power BI enhanced report format" in a Power BI Team blog post (June 2024). (Source: Microsoft Power BI blog)
- PBIR stores report metadata as structured JSON inside a `definition/` folder of a PBIP project. Microsoft explicitly references the `definition` folder in the announcement. (Source: Microsoft Power BI blog)
- PBIR is a metadata/layout format used within PBIP projects (developer-mode). The canonical user-facing file extensions for reports remain PBIX (single-file report) and PBIP (project). PBIR is not a new standalone top-level file that replaces PBIX in normal authoring workflows. (Source: Microsoft blog + project workflow context)
- Upgrading an existing PBIP to PBIR is supported via Power BI Desktop (Upgrade during save) but Microsoft warns the upgrade is irreversible for that saved file and to back up before upgrading. (Source: Microsoft blog)
- PBIR is intentionally Git-friendly (text diffs, smaller granular files), but Microsoft and local references warn that manual JSON edits are brittle and recommend schema-aware editors and PBIR-aware tooling. (Sources: Microsoft blog; PBIR Format Reference)
- There is limited or no official Microsoft documentation beyond the blog announcement (as of 2026-04-24) for downgrade workflows or built-in recovery procedures; community best practices (backups, Git, JSON validation) are the primary recovery guidance. (Sources: Microsoft blog; local PBIR Format Reference)

---

## 3) Definitions and official status

### What is a .pbir file?

- Terminology: "PBIR" refers to the Power BI Enhanced Report format — a JSON-based, modular metadata representation for Power BI reports used when saving Power BI Project (.pbip) files with the enhanced metadata option enabled. Microsoft calls it the "Power BI enhanced report format (PBIR)" in the June 2024 blog post: "Saving your Power BI Project files (PBIP) using the Power BI Enhanced Report Format (PBIR) stores the report metadata as properly formatted JSON files ..." (Power BI Team blog, June 2024). [Link: https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24).

- Practical manifestation: When PBIR is enabled, Power BI Desktop writes a `definition/` directory inside the PBIP project that contains separate JSON files for pages, visuals, bookmarks, theme, etc. The blog explicitly notes: "... your entire report metadata will be stored in a folder named "definition":" and explains the JSON schema declaration at the top of the documents to enable editor tooling. (Power BI blog) [Link above]

- Purpose: PBIR is intended to make report metadata human-readable and machine-validated (JSON schema), enabling better Git diffs and conflict resolution during team development.

### Is .pbir an official file extension?

- Official status (conservative): Microsoft officially documents PBIR as the "enhanced report format" (preview) used when storing report metadata in PBIP projects; the announcement is authoritative for PBIR's existence and design goals (Power BI blog, June 2024). However, Microsoft does not advertise PBIR as a new top-level report file extension that replaces PBIX. Instead, PBIR is the internal metadata format used within PBIP projects (the PBIP remains the project container). The Microsoft blog and announcement talk about storing metadata in a `definition/` folder and referencing a JSON schema — they do not declare a standalone user-facing file type named `.pbir` that is distributed independently of project containers.

- Conservative conclusion: PBIR is an official Microsoft metadata/layout format used within PBIP projects (as part of a preview feature). It should be described as a metadata format (a set of JSON files inside `definition/`) rather than as a broadly-distributed top-level file extension intended to replace .pbix/.pbit. (Source: Power BI blog, June 2024). [Link: https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24).

---

## 4) Side-by-side comparison table (.pbir vs .pbix vs .pbit vs .rdl vs .pbip)

Notes: The table treats PBIR as a metadata format (within PBIP) rather than a standalone top-level format; assertions are conservative and backed by the Microsoft announcement (PBIR blog). When I cite "Microsoft blog" below, it refers to the June 2024 Power BI Team blog post. Additional common knowledge about PBIX/PBIT/RDL workflows is standard Power BI behavior.

| .pbip | Power BI Project container | Project container/folder or package containing assets, model files (e.g., TMDL), and `definition/` (PBIR) when enabled | Yes (project files and PBIR JSON are editable) | High (designed for Git repos) | Depends on project layout (model may be in TMDL files) | Developer-mode project development, Git-based workflows | Power BI Desktop (Developer Mode), Git, editors | PBIP + PBIR is the recommended developer workflow for source-control-friendly report development (Power BI blog) [https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24)

References: Microsoft Power BI blog (June 2024) for PBIR design and PBIP integration. Standard PBIX/PBIT/RDL descriptions are consistent with Microsoft product documentation and widely accepted Power BI practices.

## 5) Creation / opening / conversion (stepwise, with citations)

This section provides explicit, verifiable steps for enabling PBIR, creating PBIP with PBIR, opening PBIR-backed projects, and conversion guidance. Steps cite Microsoft blog where possible. If a step is not documented by Microsoft, the report states that explicitly.

A. Enabling PBIR in Power BI Desktop (official steps)

1. Open Power BI Desktop. (Microsoft blog assumes Desktop context.)
2. Go to File > Options and settings > Options > Preview features. Check the box next to "Store reports using enhanced metadata format (PBIR)". (Microsoft Power BI blog: "Before giving it a try, you must first enable it in Power BI Desktop: go to File > Options and settings > Options > Preview features and check the box next to 'Store reports using enhanced metadata format (PBIR)'.") [Link: https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24).
3. Restart Power BI Desktop if prompted. (Microsoft blog: "After enabling the preview feature, when saving as PBIP, your entire report metadata will be stored in a folder named \"definition\":") [Link above].

B. Creating a PBIP project that uses PBIR (officially described)

1. With PBIR enabled as above, create a new Power BI Project or open an existing PBIP project in Developer Mode in Power BI Desktop. (Microsoft blog context.)
2. Save your project as PBIP. If the project is saved with PBIR enabled, Power BI Desktop will write the `definition/` folder and modular JSON metadata. (Microsoft blog: "Saving your Power BI Project files (PBIP) using the Power BI Enhanced Report Format (PBIR) stores the report metadata as properly formatted JSON files... your entire report metadata will be stored in a folder named \"definition\".") [Link above].
3. To upgrade an existing PBIP project saved in legacy format: during Save As, select "Upgrade" when prompted. Microsoft explicitly documents the upgrade flow and warns that "the upgrade to PBIR is irreversible, so please save a backup of your PBIP files in case you want to go back to PBIR-Legacy (report.json) format." (Power BI blog) [Link above].

C. Opening PBIR-backed projects (officially supported path)

- Use Power BI Desktop (with PBIR preview feature enabled) to open PBIP projects saved with PBIR. The Microsoft blog indicates Desktop will read the `definition/` layout and reconstruct the report: "When saving as PBIP, your entire report metadata will be stored in a folder named \"definition\": Existing PBIP files can also be easily upgraded to PBIR by selecting 'Upgrade' during the save operation." (Power BI blog) [Link above].

- Microsoft does not document editing PBIR files directly in the Power BI Service; the desktop is the primary authoring surface for PBIR-backed projects. The blog implies Desktop is the authoring environment and suggests backing up before upgrade. (Power BI blog) [Link above].

D. Conversion methods (official guidance and limitations)

- Legacy PBIP -> PBIR: Supported via Power BI Desktop upgrade save flow (official). Microsoft explicitly documents the "Upgrade" option when saving as PBIP and warns the upgrade is irreversible: "Warning: the upgrade to PBIR is irreversible, so please save a backup of your PBIP files in case you want to go back to PBIR-Legacy (report.json) format." (Power BI blog) [Link above].

- PBIR -> Legacy PBIP / PBIX: No official Microsoft-documented automatic downgrade path is described in the public announcement. Microsoft explicitly warns upgrade is irreversible for that saved file; it therefore recommends maintaining backups or source-control snapshots if you need the legacy format. Conservative statement: "No official downgrade path documented by Microsoft in the announcement — revert via backups or source-control." (Power BI blog) [Link above].

- PBIX <-> PBIP: Standard Power BI Desktop operations determine whether you author in single-file PBIX or project mode PBIP; Microsoft blog focuses on projects (PBIP) and PBIR when developer mode is used. For moving content between PBIX and PBIP, use Power BI Desktop's export/save workflows — Microsoft does not provide a PBIR-specific command-line conversion in the announcement. If you need programmatic conversion, no official public docs were provided in the announcement; this is a workspace for future tooling. (Power BI blog; no separate doc for conversion published in announcement) [Link above].

E. Example validation commands (community tooling / best-practice examples)

- Validate JSON files in `definition/` using jq or jsonlint (community tools):

```bash
# Install jq (Linux/Mac) and check files
for f in definition/*.json; do jq . "$f" || echo "Invalid JSON in $f"; done

# Using jsonlint (node package)
jsonlint definition/definition.pbir
```

- Use Git to inspect history and revert changes:

```bash
git status
git diff -- definition/pages/page-1.json
git checkout --ours definition/pages/page-1.json
git checkout --theirs definition/pages/page-1.json
```

Note: Microsoft recommends using schema-aware editors (the blog mentions JSON schema declaration enabling IntelliSense/validation) and using PBIR-aware tooling. The example commands above are community-standard JSON/Git tools; Microsoft does not publish CLI examples for PBIR in the announcement. (Power BI blog: JSON schema and editor guidance) [Link above].

## 6) Compatibility matrix

Legend: R = Read, W = Write, * = conditional or limited; "Desktop-first" indicates authoring primarily in Power BI Desktop.

|---|---:|---:|---|
| Power BI Desktop (Developer Mode, supported versions) | R | W | Full read/write when PBIR preview is enabled; Desktop is the canonical authoring surface for PBIR. | Power BI Desktop (Standard mode, older versions) | Partial/depends | Partial/depends | Older or standard Desktop versions that do not have the PBIR preview feature will not write PBIR-savvy `definition/` folders. Use Developer Mode with a supported version. (Power BI blog) |
| Power BI Service (app.powerbi.com) | R (published artifacts) | No (cannot author PBIR metadata in service) | Publish from Desktop: the Service hosts the resulting published report; Service does not expose PBIR JSON editing. Microsoft focuses PBIR editing on Desktop. (Microsoft blog implication) |
| Power BI Report Server | No (RDL-based) | No | PBIR is unrelated to paginated RDL-based workflows used by Report Server. Rebuild/republish paginated reports if needed. |
| Power BI Embedded | R (published content) | No (authoring via Desktop) | Publish PBIR-backed reports from Desktop to workspace, then embed the published report. |
| Power BI Fabric (Git integration / DevOps workflows) | R/W via repo + Desktop | W via repo + Desktop | PBIR is explicitly intended to improve the source-control experience (Git). Microsoft referenced this benefit in the blog: "If you save your PBIP files in a Git repository, using PBIR will enable granular tracking of every report change." (Power BI blog) [Link above]. |

Conservative note: Microsoft documentation (blog) frames PBIR as a Desktop/project-level feature for improved source-control and co-development. There are no published Microsoft docs (in the announcement) that describe direct Service-side editing of PBIR JSON; the recommended flow remains Desktop => publish to Service/Embedded.

---

## 7) Typical use cases

- Team development with Git: Put PBIP projects (with PBIR `definition/`) in Git to get fine-grained diffs and easier merge resolution.
- CI validation: Run JSON schema validation on `definition/*.json` in CI before allowing merges to main branch.
- Bulk metadata changes: Use PBIR-aware tooling or scripted JSON edits to apply consistent themes or visual property changes across pages (with careful validation).
- Review and audit: Use code-review workflows to inspect JSON diffs for changes to pages/visuals/bookmarks.
- Automation and templating: Build tools that generate or modify PBIR JSON fragments for templated report creation (but only with strong validation and backups due to brittleness).

---

## 8) Troubleshooting & recovery

A. Preflight (before upgrading or editing PBIR JSON)

- Backup the PBIP project file or ensure it is committed to Git before enabling PBIR or performing an "Upgrade". Microsoft explicitly warns: "Warning: the upgrade to PBIR is irreversible, so please save a backup of your PBIP files in case you want to go back to PBIR-Legacy (report.json) format." (Power BI blog) [https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24).
- Ensure Power BI Desktop version supports the PBIR preview feature.
- Use a schema-aware editor (VS Code configured to the JSON schema declared in PBIR files) and set up JSON linting in your development environment.

B. Recovery steps for corrupted PBIR-backed projects (concrete commands & examples)

1. Primary recovery (recommended): Restore from Git (or other backup):

```bash
# Roll back to the last known-good commit
git checkout <commit-hash> -- .
# Or reset branch to remote main
git reset --hard origin/main
```

2. Validate JSON to locate syntax errors (community tool examples):

```bash
# Check each JSON file with jq
for f in definition/*.json; do
  echo "Checking $f"; jq . "$f" > /dev/null || echo "Invalid JSON: $f";
done

# Example single-file check
jq . definition/definition.pbir || echo "definition.pbir invalid JSON"
```

3. If syntax errors found, correct in an editor and re-run validation. Watch for UTF-8 BOM issues and trailing commas.

4. Use git to find the commit that introduced the corruption:

```bash
# Show commits that touch the definition folder
git log --definition --oneline --decorate

# Bisect to find the bad commit
git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD
git bisect good <known-good-commit>
# follow bisect instructions
```

5. If JSON files are semantically invalid (pass JSON parse but fail schema), use schema validation in VS Code or ajv (npm) / other validators:

```bash
# Example using ajv-cli (npm)
ajv validate -s /path/to/pbir-schema.json -d definition/definition.pbir
```

6. Rebuild minimal project: If specific page JSON is corrupt and unrecoverable, create a new PBIP project and reconstruct only the necessary components, then reimport or copy validated JSON fragments (careful with IDs).

C. Tools and community guidance

- Microsoft: The Power BI blog warns PBIR is brittle and advises using PBIR-aware tooling and to back up before upgrade. The blog's text is the primary Microsoft guidance for PBIR behavior and safety. (Power BI blog, June 2024) [https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24).

- Community: For command examples and recovery patterns, community resources (StackOverflow, Power BI/Fabric community) discuss JSON linting, git rollbacks, and manual recovery patterns. These are community best practices rather than official Microsoft-recovery docs. Example search pages (community resources):
  - StackOverflow search for PBIR: https://stackoverflow.com/search?q=pbir (accessed 2026-04-24).
  - Power BI community search: https://community.powerbi.com/search?q=PBIR (accessed 2026-04-24).
  - Fabric community search: https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/search?q=pbir (accessed 2026-04-24).

D. JSON validation tips (practical)

- Always validate JSON syntax first (jq/jsonlint). Fix parse errors before addressing schema/semantics.
- Use the JSON schema referenced in the PBIR files (if present in the `$schema` property) and enable schema-based validation in VS Code.
- Avoid manual changes to IDs or references unless you fully understand relationships; duplicates or missing references commonly cause errors.

Conservative note: Microsoft provides limited public recovery guidance in the PBIR announcement beyond recommending backups and PBIR-aware tooling. Most detailed recovery workflows are community-derived best practices.

---

## 9) Limitations, risks, best practices

Limitations and risks

- Manual edits to PBIR JSON are brittle and can corrupt reports — Microsoft warns about this risk in the announcement. (Power BI blog) [https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/power-bi-enhanced-report-format-pbir-in-power-bi-desktop-developer-mode-preview/] (accessed 2026-04-24).
- Upgrade to PBIR is irreversible for the saved PBIP file via the Desktop workflow; maintain backups or source-control snapshots to recover the legacy layout.
- Some parts of the Power BI ecosystem (Service, Report Server) do not provide PBIR metadata editing; Desktop remains the primary editing surface.

Best practices

- Always back up the PBIP file or commit to Git before upgrading to PBIR.
- Use PBIR-aware CLI/tools (if available) and schema-aware editors (VS Code) rather than ad-hoc JSON edits.
- Integrate JSON schema validation into CI pipelines to prevent invalid metadata from being merged.
- Limit direct JSON edits and perform small, incremental changes validated locally before committing.

PBIR (Power BI Enhanced Report Format) — Concise Explanation

Executive summary
- PBIR is an enhanced metadata format for Power BI reports that stores report metadata as multiple structured JSON files (per page, per visual, bookmarks, etc.) instead of a single large JSON blob. 
- It was introduced in Power BI Desktop Developer Mode (preview) to make report metadata source-control friendly (granular diffs, easier merges, editor validation via JSON Schema). PBIR is used primarily in project-based workflows (PBIP) and Microsoft has signaled it will become the default metadata format in future releases.

Key concepts
- PBIR is about metadata representation; PBIX/PBIT/PBIP are packaging/project types.
  - PBIX: traditional Power BI report file (ZIP-like archive) containing binary model artifacts and report metadata.
  - PBIT: template format (usually excludes imported data).
  - PBIP: Power BI project folder format (used in Developer Mode) intended for source control; PBIR stores its JSON metadata inside the PBIP project's /definition folder.
- PBIR separates report-level metadata into multiple JSON files with $schema references so editors (e.g., VS Code) can validate and provide IntelliSense.

Technical highlights
- Packaging: PBIX files are ZIP-based archives. PBIP (project) is a folder; when PBIR is enabled the report metadata appears under /definition in the project.
- Files & layout (typical):
  - /definition/report.json — report-level metadata
  - /definition/pages/page-*.json — one file per page
  - /definition/visuals or per-page visual files — metadata for visuals
  - /definition/bookmarks/bookmark-*.json — bookmarks
  - /model or /semanticModel — model artifacts or TMDL (if model is text-represented)
  - Binary model artifacts (the tabular model) remain binary (not JSON) and are separate from PBIR metadata.
- Schema: PBIR JSON files include $schema links (use them in editors to validate). Exact schema URLs/version are embedded in saved files—use those for precise validation.

Use cases and workflows
- Developer/team workflows: PBIR + PBIP is intended for Git-based development, enabling granular diffs and CI/CD.
- Power BI Desktop: enable Developer Mode preview option "Store reports using enhanced metadata format (PBIR)" and save as a Power BI project (PBIP) to adopt PBIR.
- Power BI Service / Report Server: publishing/import behavior depends on the target server/service support; verify compatibility before wide adoption.

How to inspect, open, and convert
- Inspect PBIX: treat as an archive — use unzip or 7-Zip to extract contents (read-only is safest).
  - unzip report.pbix -d ./report_contents
  - 7z x report.pbix -o./report_contents
- PBIR in PBIP: open the project folder in an editor (VS Code) and inspect /definition JSON files.
- Enable PBIR in Power BI Desktop:
  - File → Options and settings → Options → Preview features → enable "Store reports using enhanced metadata format (PBIR)"
  - Save as PBIP and accept the upgrade (backup first; upgrade may be irreversible).
- Community tooling (examples; verify current syntax in tool docs):
  - pbi-tools extract report.pbix --out ./report_folder
  - pbi-tools pack ./report_folder -o report_new.pbix
- Safe editing: always back up, validate JSON against $schema, avoid manual edits to binary model artifacts, prefer tooling (pbi-tools, Tabular Editor, TMDL flows).

Security considerations
- PBIR exposes textual metadata (Power Query M scripts, connection strings, queries) making it easier to accidentally commit sensitive info.
- Credentials: Power BI Desktop and Service use secure stores; credentials are typically not stored in plain text in PBIX/PBIP, but hard-coded credentials in M scripts would be visible in PBIR—avoid this.
- Never commit secrets into source control. Use parameterization, environment configs, service principals or managed identities and gateways for production credentials.
- Run static checks on PBIR JSON and M files before committing to detect secrets or PII.

Tooling ecosystem
- Official: Power BI Desktop (Developer Mode), Power BI Service, Power BI Report Server (support varies by version).
- Model tooling: Tabular Editor and TMDL workflows for model source control.
- Community: pbi-tools, pbix inspector projects, and editor JSON schema integrations (VS Code) help automate extract/pack and validate PBIR artifacts.
- Automation: Power BI REST APIs operate at package level (PBIX import/export) and are common in CI/CD pipelines that use PBIR+PBIP source repos to build PBIXs for deployment.

Compatibility & versioning
- PBIR started as a preview feature and Microsoft announced plans to make it the default metadata format; schema and tooling are evolving.
- Keep Power BI Desktop versions consistent across teams; PBIR-enabled project files may be incompatible with older Desktop or server versions.
- Check schema version in the PBIR files and Microsoft release notes when upgrading.

Practical recommendations (short)
- Use PBIR for team development with Git; enable Developer Mode and adopt PBIP workflows.
- Backup before upgrading to PBIR and before manual edits.

Second, to get the previous year's YTD for the exact same period, wrap the first measure in `CALCULATE` and apply the `SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR` or `DATEADD` function: `CALCULATE([Sales YTD], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))`. actual_output:
# YTD Sales and Year-over-Year Comparison in DAX — Practical Guide

## Executive summary

This guide gives a focused, actionable recipe to calculate Year-to-Date (YTD) sales and compare it to the exact same period last year (YTD LY) using DAX. It includes prerequisites, copy-pasteable sample tables, recommended DAX measures (TOTALYTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR and DATEADD variants), fiscal-year alternatives, expected numeric outputs for the sample data, guidance on filter context/visual interactions, common pitfalls with fixes, and debugging tips with DAX Studio patterns.

Use these short, tested patterns when you need reliable YTD and YTD LY results inside visuals or measures. Prefer a well-formed Date table marked as Date Table and use TOTALYTD where possible for simplicity; use DATEADD-based alternatives when you need strict month-to-month alignment (e.g., leap-year handling or custom calendars).

---

## Prerequisites

- Date table requirements
  - A dedicated Date table with a continuous date range covering the years of interest (no missing days within the used range).
  - A column of type Date (commonly 'Date'[Date]) with unique values.
  - Common helpful columns: Year, MonthNumber, MonthName, FiscalYear, FiscalMonth, IsWorkday (optional).
- Relationships
  - A single (one-to-many) relationship from 'Date'[Date] -> Sales[Date] (Date table side is the one side).
- Mark as Date Table
  - Mark the Date table as the model's Date table (Modeling -> Mark as date table) referencing the Date column.
- Continuous date range
  - Date table must be continuous for all days required by time intelligence (or continuous per fiscal year if using only fiscal ranges).

---

## Small example dataset (copy-pasteable CSVs)

Date table (month-end entries used for brevity; a real model should contain daily rows):

Date,Year,MonthNumber,MonthName,FiscalYear
2023-01-31,2023,1,January,2023
2023-02-28,2023,2,February,2023
2023-03-31,2023,3,March,2023
2023-04-30,2023,4,April,2023
2023-05-31,2023,5,May,2023
2023-06-30,2023,6,June,2023
2023-07-31,2023,7,July,2023
2024-01-31,2024,1,January,2024
2024-02-29,2024,2,February,2024
2024-03-31,2024,3,March,2024
2024-04-30,2024,4,April,2024
2024-05-31,2024,5,May,2024
2024-06-30,2024,6,June,2024
2024-07-31,2024,7,July,2024

Sales table (one row per month-end date; SalesAmount numeric):

Date,SalesAmount
2023-01-31,1000
2023-02-28,1200
2023-03-31,1300
2023-04-30,1100
2023-05-31,1500
2023-06-30,1400
2023-07-31,1600
2024-01-31,1100
2024-02-29,1250
2024-03-31,1350
2024-04-30,1150
2024-05-31,1550
2024-06-30,1450
2024-07-31,1700

(Notes: for real models use daily Date table; this example uses month-end rows to keep the sample small while illustrating YTD accumulation and Feb 29.)

---

## Recommended DAX measures (purpose, code, short explanation)

1) Total Sales

Purpose: base measure to sum sales values.

```
Total Sales = SUM( Sales[SalesAmount] )
```

One-line explanation: Simple aggregation used by all time-intel measures.

2) YTD Sales (TOTALYTD)

Purpose: current year-to-date sales (calendar YTD) — recommended when you have a normal calendar and a proper Date table.

```
YTD Sales =
TOTALYTD(
    [Total Sales],
    'Date'[Date]
)
```

One-line explanation: Uses DATESYTD under the covers to sum from year start through the last date in the current filter context.

3) YTD Sales (SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR variant)

Purpose: get YTD for the prior year by shifting the YTD window back one year using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR.

```
YTD Sales LY (SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR) =
CALCULATE(
    [YTD Sales],
    SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR( 'Date'[Date] )
)
```

One-line explanation: Calculates YTD amounts but with the date window shifted one year back; simple and common pattern.

4) YTD Sales LY (DATEADD alternative)

Purpose: alternate way to compute YTD LY using DATEADD — useful when you want strict behavior that shifts by years/months and helps with some leap-year/month alignment concerns.

```
YTD Sales LY (DATEADD) =
CALCULATE(
    [YTD Sales],
    DATEADD( 'Date'[Date], -1, YEAR )
)
```

One-line explanation: Shifts the evaluation context back exactly 1 year (using date arithmetic) then evaluates the same YTD window; sometimes preferred for custom calendars/strict month matching.

5) Percent Change (YoY %) — YTD

Purpose: measure the percentage change between current YTD and YTD LY.

```
YTD YoY % =
VAR Curr = [YTD Sales]
VAR Prev = [YTD Sales LY (DATEADD)]
RETURN
DIVIDE( Curr - Prev, Prev, 0 )
```

One-line explanation: Uses DIVIDE to avoid divide-by-zero; choose which YTD LY variant (SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or DATEADD) depending on required behavior.

6) Fiscal YTD variations

Purpose: calculate fiscal-year-to-date when fiscal year does not start on Jan 1.

- TOTALYTD with YearEndDate parameter (example: fiscal year ends June 30):

```
Fiscal YTD (TOTALYTD year-end Jun30) =
TOTALYTD(
    [Total Sales],
    'Date'[Date],
    ,
    "6/30"
)
```

One-line explanation: TOTALYTD supports an optional YearEndDate argument ("MM/DD" string in many locales); this shifts YTD accumulation so the fiscal year starts July 1 and ends June 30.

- Alternative: Fiscal YTD using FiscalYear column (recommended when you have explicit FiscalYear or FiscalStartMonth data):

```
Fiscal YTD (by FiscalYear column) =
VAR CurrentDate = MAX( 'Date'[Date] )
VAR FY = MAX( 'Date'[FiscalYear] )
RETURN
CALCULATE(
    [Total Sales],
    FILTER(
        ALL( 'Date' ),
        'Date'[FiscalYear] = FY
            && 'Date'[Date] <= CurrentDate
    )
)
```

One-line explanation: Filters the Date table to the current fiscal year up to the current date; reliable for arbitrary fiscal definitions.

(Choose the approach that matches your model: TOTALYTD YearEndDate is concise; using FiscalYear column is explicit and portable across locales.)

---

## Example outputs (expected numeric outputs for the sample dataset)

Using the sample data above, evaluate the measures for context = 2024-07-31 (selecting final date of July 2024):

- Total Sales (for 2024-07-31 row): 1,700 (value for that date only)
- YTD Sales (calendar) — sum Jan–Jul 2024:
  - 2024 YTD = 1100 + 1250 + 1350 + 1150 + 1550 + 1450 + 1700 = 9,550
- YTD Sales LY (same period last year) — sum Jan–Jul 2023:
  - 2023 YTD = 1000 + 1200 + 1300 + 1100 + 1500 + 1400 + 1600 = 9,100
- YTD YoY % = (9,550 - 9,100) / 9,100 = 450 / 9,100 = 0.04945 → 4.95%

Tabular expected output (copy-pasteable):

ContextDate,TotalSales,YTD Sales,YTD Sales LY,YTD YoY %
2024-07-31,1700,9550,9100,4.945%
2023-07-31,1600,9100, -- (previous-year-of-2022 not present) -- , --

(If you present a matrix by month, [YTD Sales] will show the cumulative value up to that month.)

---

## Notes on filter context and visuals

- Last date in the filter context is critical: TOTALYTD and DATESYTD use the last date visible in the current filter context to define the YTD window. In visuals, that typically means:
  - For a matrix or table with one row per Month, the YTD value on the Jul row is computed to include all months up to Jul.
  - For a time-series line chart, the YTD point at July corresponds to the cumulative sum up to that date.
- Slicers and page filters affect the filter context. If a slicer restricts the Date table to a subset, the YTD window is computed relative to the filtered dates. Avoid unintentionally limiting the date range with slicers when you expect full-year accumulation.
- If you multi-select non-contiguous months in a slicer (e.g., Mar and Jul), DATESYTD/TOTALYTD may produce a YTD window that corresponds to the maximum date in the selection and include dates between the year start and that maximum date — which can be unexpected. Prefer single selection or explicit logic.
- Visual-level filters vs measure-level filters: CALCULATE inside a measure can override filters; validate filter precedence when layering filters in visuals.

---

## Common pitfalls and fixes (symptom -> cause -> fix)

1) Symptom: YTD shows blank or small values
   - Cause: Date table not marked as Date Table or relationship missing/wrong direction.
   - Fix: Create one-to-many relationship Date[Date] -> Sales[Date] and Mark as Date Table.

2) Symptom: YTD seems to skip months or not cumulative
   - Cause: Missing dates (non-continuous Date table) or model uses month-only rows.
   - Fix: Use a continuous daily Date table covering the full range; repopulate missing days.

3) Symptom: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR returns unexpected day for Feb 29 (maps to Mar 1)
   - Cause: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR has calendar semantics that can shift Feb 29 to Mar 1 in some contexts.
   - Fix: Use DATEADD-based variant (DATEADD('Date'[Date], -1, YEAR)) or compare by month-level grouping if you want Feb-to-Feb mapping.

4) Symptom: Merge conflicts or inconsistent results across developer machines
   - Cause: Different Power BI Desktop / model versions or schema differences in Date table.
   - Fix: Standardize Desktop versions, use a single marked Date table design, and store model artifacts in source control.

5) Symptom: Duplicate dates error or ambiguous relationships
   - Cause: Date table contains duplicate Date values or Sales[Date] has time component.
   - Fix: Ensure Date column values are unique and of Date type; strip time components (use DateTime -> Date conversion).

6) Symptom: Performance degradation on large models with complex calculations
   - Cause: Inefficient filters or unnecessary use of ALL in measure that expands cardinality.
   - Fix: Optimize by restricting FILTER to necessary columns, use variables to cache intermediate results, consider aggregations.

7) Symptom: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR not allowed in calculated column or RLS in DirectQuery
   - Cause: Function not supported in those contexts for DirectQuery.
   - Fix: Implement time-intel as measures (not calculated columns) and test in import mode where possible; use alternative expressions supported in DirectQuery.

---

## Debugging checklist and DAX Studio / tools tips

- Quick sanity checks inside Power BI / measure tests:
  - Check Date table continuity: MIN('Date'[Date]) and MAX('Date'[Date]) and COUNTROWS('Date').
  - Confirm unique dates: COUNTROWS( 'Date' ) = COUNTROWS( DISTINCT( 'Date'[Date] ) )
  - Relationship check: ensure relationship exists and is active.
- Helper measures to inspect filter context:

```
SelectedDatesCount = COUNTROWS( VALUES( 'Date'[Date] ) )
MinDateInContext = MIN( 'Date'[Date] )
MaxDateInContext = MAX( 'Date'[Date] )
```

- DAX Studio tips
  - Use EVALUATE to inspect measure outputs across dates:

```
EVALUATE
ADDCOLUMNS(
    FILTER( 'Date', 'Date'[Date] <= DATE(2024,7,31) ),
    "TotalSales", [Total Sales],
    "YTD", [YTD Sales],
    "YTD_LY", [YTD Sales LY (DATEADD)]
)
ORDER BY 'Date'[Date]
```

  - Use Server Timings and Query Plan in DAX Studio to diagnose slow measures.
  - Use Query Builder to visualize the sets returned by DAX functions (e.g., DATESYTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR) by wrapping them in EVALUATE.

- Useful debugging expressions
  - Check what SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR returns in context:

```
EVALUATE
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR( DATESINPERIOD( 'Date'[Date], DATE(2024,2,29), 1, DAY ) )
```

  - Validate DATESYTD set:

```
EVALUATE
DATESYTD( 'Date'[Date], "12/31" )
```

(Adjust examples as needed; EVALUATE clauses run in DAX Studio against your model.)

---

## Authoritative references & suggested search phrases

(Authoritative topics to search or read):

- Microsoft Learn / DAX reference pages:
  - TOTALYTD function (DAX)
  - SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR function (DAX)
  - DATEADD function (DAX)
- SQLBI: articles about Time Intelligence in DAX and best practices, including leap-year considerations and fiscal calendars.
- dax.guide: concise reference for DAX time-intel functions (TOTALYTD, DATESYTD, DATEADD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR).

Suggested search phrases:
- "TOTALYTD DAX Microsoft Learn"
- "SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR leap year behavior"
- "DATEADD DAX vs SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR"
- "DAX fiscal year TOTALYTD YearEndDate"
- "YTD Sales DAX example"

---

## Appendix: advanced notes

- Leap year handling
  - SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR has specific calendar semantics that may shift Feb 29 to Mar 1; DATEADD can be used to force a shift by exact years or months depending on needs.
- Non-standard calendars
  - For 4-4-5, 13-period, or other week-based calendars, you must implement custom time-intelligence (do not rely on built-in functions). Build fiscal-period columns and use FILTER/ALL patterns as shown in the Fiscal YTD by FiscalYear example.
- Performance
  - Prefer minimizing row-by-row FILTERs over large tables in measures. Use CALCULATE with a table expression that the engine can optimize (e.g., DATESYTD, DATEADD).
  - Use variables to store repeated results in a measure to avoid duplicated computation.

---

## Quick reference cheat-sheet (copyable)

- Base measure:
```
Total Sales = SUM( Sales[SalesAmount] )
```
- Calendar YTD:
```
YTD Sales = TOTALYTD( [Total Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
```
- YTD LY (simple):
```
YTD Sales LY = CALCULATE( [YTD Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR( 'Date'[Date] ) )
```
- YTD LY (DATEADD):
```
YTD Sales LY (DATEADD) = CALCULATE( [YTD Sales], DATEADD( 'Date'[Date], -1, YEAR ) )
```
- YTD % change:
```
YTD YoY % = DIVIDE( [YTD Sales] - [YTD Sales LY (DATEADD)], [YTD Sales LY (DATEADD)], 0 )
```
- Fiscal YTD (using FiscalYear column):
```
Fiscal YTD =
VAR CurrentDate = MAX( 'Date'[Date] )
VAR FY = MAX( 'Date'[FiscalYear] )
RETURN
CALCULATE( [Total Sales], FILTER( ALL( 'Date' ), 'Date'[FiscalYear] = FY && 'Date'[Date] <= CurrentDate ) )
```

---

End of report

The core advantages include: 1) **Source Control Integration:** Teams can track granular changes to visuals and pages using Git. 2) **Concurrent Development:** Multiple developers can work on the same report simultaneously without file locking, resolving conflicts via standard Pull Request processes. 3) **CI/CD Pipelines:** PBIR files can be easily validated, programmatically modified (e.g., swapping out theme files or data sources), and deployed using Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions via Fabric Git Integration.
actual_output:
# PBIR vs PBIX: Core Advantages for Enterprise Teams — Revised

> Note (explicit): This report is printed here only and will not be saved to any external system by the author of this report. It is presented solely in this output.

## Executive summary

This updated report expands the prior analysis of PBIR (Power BI Enhanced Report metadata, typically used inside Power BI Project / PBIP workflows) versus PBIX (the legacy single-file package) to address enterprise concerns raised in the critique: official Microsoft announcement and timeline for PBIR, compatibility across Power BI Desktop versions and the Power BI Service REST API, embedding and Power BI Report Server status, migration tooling (pbi-tools and CLIs), licensing implications, governance/compliance and backup/DR recommendations for folder-based projects, and expanded CI/CD examples using community tooling.

Summary conclusions (high-level):
- Microsoft has announced and begun rolling out PBIR as the default enhanced report metadata format in the Power BI Service and is transitioning Desktop to use PBIR inside PBIX/PBIP. This is a platform-level change that improves source-control and CI/CD support (official Microsoft blog/announcement). [Sources below]
- PBIP (Power BI Project) that uses PBIR metadata is the recommended team-development surface: it enables text-based diffs, code review, and automation. PBIX remains the single-file artifact for many deployment scenarios and will internally adopt PBIR-format metadata (so REST API import continues to work with PBIX files). [Microsoft Learn; Power BI Blog]
- Use a hybrid pattern: develop and review in PBIP/PBIR (Git), run CI validation and produce release PBIX or import payloads, and deploy via REST API or CLI to workspaces or Report Server as required.

This revised report provides: official timeline references, compatibility notes, commands and examples for pbi-tools and CLI patterns, expanded CI/CD pipelines, governance and backup/DR guidance for folder-based projects, licensing implications, and a refreshed set of references.

---

## Quick comparison (short)

| Topic | PBIR (text metadata; PBIP workflows) | PBIX (single-file package) |
|---|---:|---|
| Official Microsoft status & timeline | Announced as new default for Power BI Service starting Jan 2026; rollout ongoing; Desktop adoption phased (see blog timeline). PBIR will become standard metadata inside PBIX/PBIP. [Power BI Blog] | PBIX remains the primary developer file type; PBIX will use PBIR-format metadata internally after transition. [Power BI Blog] |
| Source control & collaboration | Best: text files → Git diffs, PRs, merge conflict resolution, code review. [Microsoft Learn] | Poorly suited to Git as binary; teams often use locking or artifact-level workflows. |
| CI/CD & automation | Designed for CI/CD: validate JSON, run linters, programmatic edits, generate artifacts. [Microsoft Learn] | Deployable via REST API import; automation typically uploads a PBIX file. |
| REST API / embedding / Report Server | PBIX imports supported by REST API. PBIR metadata will be inside PBIX; embedding continues to work. Report Server on-premises status requires specific migration planning. | Well-supported by REST API import endpoints; embedding unchanged functionally. |

---

## Official Microsoft announcement & timeline (PBIR)

Key official source (Power BI team blog): "PBIR will become the default Power BI report format — Get ready for the transition" (Power BI Blog). The blog documents the rollout schedule and provides status updates:
- Rollout to Power BI Service started January 2026, with staged automatic conversion behavior for existing service reports when edited and saved. The service conversion rollout is staged by report size (examples noted). The blog contains updates indicating Desktop adoption timing was adjusted in follow-up updates. [Power BI Blog]
- The blog explicitly states PBIX files will continue to be the primary file format for authors, but the internal metadata inside PBIX/PBIP will move to the PBIR enhanced metadata format, making PBIX and PBIP contain PBIR metadata. [Power BI Blog]

Why this matters: Microsoft’s announcement and timeline are the authoritative confirmation that PBIR is mainstreaming and that teams should plan to adopt PBIR/PBIP development practices (Git + CI) while accounting for phased rollout and conversion behaviors.

Source:
- Power BI Blog: PBIR will become the default Power BI report format — Get ready for the transition. https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/pbir-will-become-the-default-power-bi-report-format-get-ready-for-the-transition/

---

## Compatibility and platform support

This section covers compatibility across Power BI Desktop releases, Power BI Service (REST API import), embedding, and Power BI Report Server (on-premises) status.

1) Power BI Desktop versions
- Microsoft’s blog and documentation indicate PBIR support and PBIP project workflows are available in Desktop (PBIP preview), with GA timelines for PBIP/PBIR evolving during 2025–2026. Desktop included PBIR preview options; Microsoft communicated phased adoption and release-date updates in the blog. Administrators and authors should confirm their Desktop release version and whether the PBIP/Enhanced Report preview settings are required to author in PBIR.
- Practical implication: For teams using PBIP/PBIR in source control, require developer machines to run a supported Power BI Desktop build (the blog references specific release windows — check Desktop release notes before enforcing a minimum version).

Source:
- Power BI Blog (timeline and Desktop notes): https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/pbir-will-become-the-default-power-bi-report-format-get-ready-for-the-transition/
- Microsoft Learn — Power BI Desktop projects (PBIP): https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/developer/projects/projects-overview

2) Power BI Service REST API import support
- The Power BI REST API supports importing PBIX artifacts to workspaces (Import API). Because PBIX will contain PBIR metadata internally after transition, the import flow using a PBIX is preserved. Use the Import endpoint for programmatic uploads: Post Import (in group) REST API. [REST API docs]
- Practical implication: CI/CD pipelines that upload PBIX via REST API should continue to function. When using PBIP in source control, include a build step to produce/update a PBIX artifact for REST API import if required by your deployment flow.

Source:
- Power BI REST API — Imports: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/power-bi/imports/post-import-in-group
- Power BI Blog (notes that PBIX will remain the primary file format and that change is internal to metadata): https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/pbir-will-become-the-default-power-bi-report-format-get-ready-for-the-transition/

3) Embedding support (report embedding / application embedding)
- Because PBIR describes metadata and the report packaging semantics remain supported (PBIX or service-hosted reports), embedding existing reports via the Power BI embedding APIs or SDKs should continue to work. Microsoft’s messaging describes PBIX remaining the primary file type and that change is largely transparent to runtime. However, test embedding flows in a pre-production environment during migration to verify token acquisition, dataset/report IDs, and behavior across SDK versions.

Sources:
- Power BI Blog (runtime that PBIX remains primary and is a silent upgrade): https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/pbir-will-become-the-default-power-bi-report-format-get-ready-for-the-transition/
- Power BI embedding docs (refer to embedding guides in Microsoft docs for your SDK version).

4) Power BI Report Server (on-premises)
- On-premises Power BI Report Server (PBIRS) is a separate product; Microsoft has continued to evolve on-premises support paths. Migration and conversion for on-premises reports requires extra planning. Microsoft documentation and Report Server release notes should be consulted for exact support windows and whether PBIR metadata is supported in your PBIRS version.
- Practical implication: Don’t assume automatic or immediate parity between Power BI Service and Report Server; plan explicit compatibility testing and a migration strategy for on-premises instances.

Sources / references:
- Power BI Report Server documentation and release notes (check the Microsoft docs relevant to your SQL Server / Report Server release) and community threads on PBIRS migration.

---

## Migration tooling and example commands (pbi-tools, CLIs)

There are community and open-source tools commonly used to extract, serialize and repackage Power BI artifacts, which help with migration and CI/CD. Two notable examples are pbi-tools (community project) and CLI utilities that simplify REST/API-based deployment.

1) pbi-tools (GitHub)
- pbi-tools is a community toolset that can extract a PBIX/PBIP into a folder structure and re-pack it. This is useful for working with the PBIR metadata in source control workflows and for programmatic changes.
- Typical commands (illustrative — always verify exact CLI flags and version on the project README):
  - Extract a PBIX into a folder structure (to source-control the content):
    - pbi-tools extract path/to/report.pbix -o ./src/ReportName
  - Pack the folder back into a PBIX for deployment:
    - pbi-tools pack ./src/ReportName -o ./artifacts/report.pbix

Notes:
- Use the pbi-tools GitHub repo README for exact installation and command syntax. pbi-tools supports extraction/serialization scenarios that align with PBIR/PBIP workflows.

Source:
- pbi-tools (GitHub): https://github.com/pbi-tools/pbi-tools

2) pbi-cli / Power BI CLI tools
- There are community and vendor CLI wrappers and official Power Platform/Power BI tooling that can be used to authenticate and call REST APIs (to import PBIX, set permissions, etc.). Example steps for deployment with a CLI or script:
  - Authenticate the CLI via service principal (client id/secret) or using a service connection.
  - Call the REST API imports endpoint or use the CLI import command to upload the PBIX to the target workspace.

Illustrative command pattern (pseudo):
  - pbi login --client-id $CLIENT_ID --client-secret $SECRET --tenant $TENANT_ID
  - pbi import --workspace-id $WORKSPACE_ID --file ./artifacts/report.pbix --name "My Report"

Notes:
- Verify exact CLI names and command-line flags on the specific CLI project you choose (community CLIs vary). Where possible prefer Microsoft-supported tooling or well-maintained community tools and pin to a tested version in CI.

Suggested tool references:
- pbi-tools (GitHub): https://github.com/pbi-tools/pbi-tools
- Search GitHub for 'Power BI CLI' or use REST API directly from scripts in CI.

---

## Licensing implications (concise)

- Publishing & workspace permissions: To publish a report to a Power BI Service workspace, authors typically require a Power BI Pro license, unless the workspace is in a Premium capacity where publishing privileges differ. Automated deployments using service principals also require tenant configuration and Power BI admin tenant settings for service principal usage. Confirm licensing and tenant policies for your organization.

- Runtime licensing: Users consuming reports in non-Premium workspaces usually need a Pro license or must access content shared in Premium capacities. Embedding scenarios (app owns data vs user owns data) also have licensing models—review Microsoft embedding and licensing docs for your chosen pattern.

- Development tooling: Power BI Desktop is free for local development, but service-level operations (publish, sharing, app workspaces) are subject to Pro/Premium licensing constraints.

Sources / references:
- Power BI licensing overview and documentation (see Microsoft Power BI licensing pages and service docs for precise rules in your tenant).
- Power BI REST API and service-principal documentation (tenant admin settings required for service principal auth).

---

## Governance, compliance, backup and DR for folder-based PBIP/PBIR projects

Folder-based projects change the operational control points. Below are recommended governance and backup/DR practices.

1) Access control and compliance
- Repository-level security: Enforce least-privilege access to Git repos that store PBIP/PBIR project files. Use role-based access and group membership controls (Azure DevOps, GitHub orgs) and protect main branches with required review policies.
- Secrets & credentials: Do not store secrets or local settings (localSettings.json, cache.abf) in Git. Use the recommended .gitignore entries (Power BI Desktop creates defaults such as **/.pbi/localSettings.json and **/.pbi/cache.abf) to prevent accidental check-ins. Centralize secrets in a vault (Azure Key Vault, GitHub Secrets) and ensure CI pipelines retrieve secrets at runtime.
- Compliance scanning: Run static checks in CI for forbidden connectors, PII exposure patterns in measure names or column descriptions, and banned custom visuals. Maintain an approved visuals list and block others in policies.

2) Backup & DR
- Use Git as the primary versioned backup for project metadata and code. Configure repository backups and retention: enable repository backups or enable Geo-redundancy where available for your hosting (Azure Repos / GitHub Enterprise backup options).
- Artifacts & release feeds: Store built PBIX artifacts in an artifact repository (Azure Artifacts, GitHub Releases, or a secure file share) with immutable versioning. Keep at least N last releases per compliance policy.
- Local caches and binary data: Because caches are ignored from Git, ensure dataset refreshes and retraining can rebuild required caches. For critical on-prem datasets, keep an archival process to store the dataset extract or data source snapshots.
- Disaster recovery runbooks: Create a DR runbook that includes steps to clone repo at a commit, build/pack with pbi-tools (or supported toolchain), and import via REST API to a standby workspace. Maintain stored service principal credentials in an emergency vault with proper approvals.

3) Audit logging and retention
- Use Git audit logs and repository activity monitoring for traceability. For deployments, use pipeline run logs and store mapping of pipeline run -> Git commit -> deployed artifact -> workspace/report IDs.

Sources / references:
- Microsoft Learn — Power BI Desktop projects (.gitignore guidance): https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/developer/projects/projects-overview
- pbi-tools GitHub (for extraction/packing patterns): https://github.com/pbi-tools/pbi-tools

---

## Expanded CI/CD examples with pbi-tools / REST API patterns

Below are example CI/CD pipeline patterns that integrate pbi-tools extraction/pack operations and use the Power BI REST API to deploy. These examples are illustrative; validate exact commands and endpoints against the tools and your organizational policies.

Example: GitHub Actions pipeline (validate -> build -> pack -> deploy)

1) Validate stage
- Run JSON schema validation and linter against PBIR files (validate structure, naming conventions). Use a container step that runs a node/python linter script.

2) Build stage (pack an artifact)
- Use pbi-tools to pack the repo folder into a PBIX artifact; this lets you check-in textual changes and still produce a PBIX for environments expecting a PBIX upload.

Steps (pseudo-YAML snippet; replace with real actions and secure secret usage):

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Setup pbi-tools
        run: |
          # install pbi-tools according to repo instructions (dotnet or binary)
          curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pbi-tools/pbi-tools/master/install.sh | bash
      - name: Pack PBIX artifact
        run: |
          # pack folder-based project into a PBIX for deployment
          pbi-tools pack ./src/ReportName -o ./artifacts/report.pbix
      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: pbix-artifact
          path: ./artifacts/report.pbix
```

3) Deploy stage (import PBIX via REST API — service principal auth)
- Authenticate with Azure AD to obtain an access token for Power BI.
- Call the Post Import (in group) endpoint to upload the PBIX to the target workspace.

Pseudo script steps:

```bash
# 1. Get AD token (example using curl & OAuth client_credentials)
ACCESS_TOKEN=$(curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$TENANT_ID/oauth2/v2.0/token" \
  -F 'grant_type=client_credentials' \
  -F "client_id=$CLIENT_ID" \
  -F "client_secret=$CLIENT_SECRET" \
  -F 'scope=https://analysis.windows.net/powerbi/api/.default' | jq -r .access_token)

# 2. Import PBIX to a workspace
curl -X POST "https://api.powerbi.com/v1.0/myorg/groups/$WORKSPACE_ID/imports?datasetDisplayName=MyReport" \
 -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
 -F "file=@./artifacts/report.pbix"
```

Notes and enhancements:
- Use GitHub Secrets / Azure Key Vault for client secrets.
- Implement smoke tests (using the Power BI REST API to query report pages or a simple embedding test) before promoting to Prod.
- For large datasets, consider incrementally deploying metadata and refreshing the dataset post-deploy.

Sources / references:
- pbi-tools GitHub repository (install and pack/extract docs): https://github.com/pbi-tools/pbi-tools
- Power BI REST API — Import endpoint: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/power-bi/imports/post-import-in-group

---

## Migration runbook (revised and condensed)

1) Inventory & classify reports.
2) Create a Git repository and protective policies; add .gitignore (include **/.pbi/cache.abf and **/.pbi/localSettings.json).
3) Convert sample PBIX to PBIP/PBIR using supported Power BI Desktop (enable PBIP preview if required) and commit the generated folder structure.
4) Use pbi-tools (or the extraction tool of choice) to validate round-trip (extract -> commit -> pack -> pbix). Example:
   - pbi-tools extract ./report.pbix -o ./src/ReportName
   - pbi-tools pack ./src/ReportName -o ./artifacts/report.pbix
5) Build and deploy in CI to a test workspace using service principal authentication and REST API import.
6) Run automated smoke tests and manual QA.
7) Promote to production with approval gates; retain old PBIX artifacts for rollback.
8) Document and train authors.

Sources / references:
- Microsoft Learn — Power BI Desktop projects: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/developer/projects/projects-overview
- pbi-tools GitHub: https://github.com/pbi-tools/pbi-tools
- Power BI Blog (PBIR rollout): https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/pbir-will-become-the-default-power-bi-report-format-get-ready-for-the-transition/

---

## Governance & compliance (expanded)

- Policy-as-code: Implement repository-level policy checks for banned connectors, disallowed visuals, or patterns that may expose PII. Run these checks in CI prior to merging.
- Audit trails: Require PR approvals and maintain pipeline logs mapping commits -> build -> deploy for compliance evidence.
- Data residency & export controls: Ensure the repo and artifacts respect organizational data residency and export control policies. If storing exported metadata with column names, classify and handle repositories accordingly.
- Retention & eDiscovery: Retain repository history and release artifacts according to regulatory retention policies and document retention-period rationale.

Sources:
- Microsoft governance guidance and enterprise best practices for source control and pipeline auditing.

---

## Backup / DR specifics for folder-based projects (practical)

- Git backups: Take scheduled backups of repositories (or use built-in geo-redundant hosting). Keep replicated replicas of Git hosting or export bundles (git bundle).
- Artifact snapshotting: Keep release artifacts (PBIXs) in a separate artifact feed with immutability and access controls.
- Rapid restore runbook: Steps to check out a specific commit and rebuild/pack artifacts, then import via REST API into a standby workspace. Keep runbook and credentials in an emergency vault.

---

## Final recommendations (concise & pragmatic)

1) Adopt PBIP/PBIR for development and source control. Use Git with branch protection and PR-based reviews.
2) Use pbi-tools or equivalent to enable extract/pack workflows; include round-trip checks in CI to ensure pack/unpack fidelity.
3) Continue to produce PBIX release artifacts for REST API-based deployments unless your pipeline supports direct PBIR import; test and automate import steps with service principal auth.
4) Harden governance: secrets vaults, forbidden-connector checks, logging and artifact retention.
5) Define backup/DR and rollback procedures that operate at repo and artifact levels.
6) Verify compliance and licensing for publishing and embedding in your tenant: Pro vs Premium licensing and service-principal tenant settings.

---

## Sources (prioritized and cited where used)

- Power BI Blog — PBIR will become the default Power BI report format — Get ready for the transition (official announcement & timeline). https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/pbir-will-become-the-default-power-bi-report-format-get-ready-for-the-transition/

- Microsoft Learn — Power BI Desktop project report folder / report considerations (PBIR and projects report considerations). https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/developer/projects/projects-report

actual_output:
# Context Transition у DAX — пояснення та мінімальний приклад

## Виконавче резюме

Context Transition (перехід контексту) — це механіка DAX, коли наявний рядковий контекст (row context) перетворюється у фільтровий контекст (filter context) під дією функції CALCULATE або CALCULATETABLE (або будь-якого виразу, що явно/неявно їх викликає). Це ключова концепція для розуміння, чому однакові вирази в обчислюваному стовпці (calculated column) та у мірі (measure) дають різні результати, а також як робити коректні агрегації по зв'язаних таблицях.

У цьому звіті: короткі визначення, мінімальний приклад з трьома рядками, DAX-код для обчислюваного стовпця та міри, детальний покроковий трасування виконання, критичний робочий кейс, контрольний список помилок при налагодженні та рекомендації.

---

## Визначення

- Row context (рядковий контекст)
  - Контекст, де формула оцінюється для конкретного рядка таблиці (наприклад, при створенні обчислюваного стовпця або всередині ітератора, такого як SUMX, FILTER, ADDCOLUMNS). Row context дає доступ до значень стовпців поточного рядка.

- Filter context (фільтровий контекст)
  - Сукупність фільтрів, які застосовані до обчислення (визначаються фільтрами візуалізації, slicer-ами, фільтрами з заходів CALCULATE тощо). Агрегатні функції (SUM, COUNT) працюють у межах фільтрового контексту.

- Context Transition (перехід контексту)
  - Коли DAX виконує CALCULATE (або CALCULATETABLE) у середовищі, де існує row context, то цей row context «перетворюється» у еквівалентні фільтри (filter context) перед тим, як виконати внутрішній вираз. Іншими словами, значення стовпців поточного рядка стають фільтрами на відповідні стовпці/пов'язані таблиці. Після цього CALCULATE застосовує свої додаткові фільтри/модифікатори.

- Роль CALCULATE
  - CALCULATE змінює filter context. Якщо викликається з наявним row context, — перш ніж застосувати модифікатори — він здійснює Context Transition: перетворює row context → filter context. Це основний механізм, який робить можливим обчислення агрегатів «по поточному рядку» у пов'язаних таблицях.

---

## Приклад даних (мінімальний)

Таблиця Products (1 рядок на продукт):

| ProductKey | ProductName |
|---:|---|
| 1 | Widget |
| 2 | Gadget |

Таблиця Sales (три рядки):

| SaleID | ProductKey | Qty |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 1 | 3 |
| 3 | 2 | 4 |

Примітки:
- Відношення: Products[ProductKey] (1) — Sales[ProductKey] (N).
- Ми хочемо в Products отримати загальну кількість продажів (TotalQty) для кожного продукту.

---

## DAX: обчислюваний стовпець vs міра

1) Обчислюваний стовпець у таблиці Products (правильний, використовує Context Transition):

```DAX
ProductTotalQty =
CALCULATE(
    SUM(Sales[Qty])
)
```

2) Спроба написати те саме без CALCULATE (НЕПРАВИЛЬНО в обчислюваному стовпці):

```DAX
ProductTotalQty_Wrong =
SUM(Sales[Qty])
```

- У обчислюваному стовпці прямий виклик SUM(Sales[Qty]) не має фільтрового контексту, а лише рядковий контекст таблиці Products — тому без CALCULATE збірка контексту для SUM не відбудеться так, як треба; зазвичай це або викликає помилку або повертає некоректні значення (залежно від середовища і способу написання). Правильний шлях — використовувати CALCULATE.

3) Міра, що повертає загальну кількість (візуально залежить від фільтрів):

```DAX
Measure_TotalQty = SUM(Sales[Qty])
```

- Міра працює у фільтровому контексті (наприклад, якщо візуал відображає ProductName, Power BI автоматично додає фільтр ProductKey=<значення> у filter context), тому для міри CALCULATE не потрібен, щоб отримати суму по продукту в рядку табличного візуалу.

---

## Очікуваний результат (таблиця Products після обчислення)

| ProductKey | ProductName | ProductTotalQty |
|---:|---|---:|
| 1 | Widget | 5 |
| 2 | Gadget | 4 |

Пояснення: для ProductKey=1 сума Qty з рядків Sales (2 + 3) = 5; для ProductKey=2 = 4.

---

## Покрокове виконання (trace) — ProductTotalQty (обчислюваний стовпець)

При обчисленні ProductTotalQty для кожного рядка таблиці Products відбувається таке:

1. Початково виконується обчислення для першого рядка Products (ProductKey = 1). Тепер у нас є row context: Products[ProductKey] = 1.
2. Вираз всередині стовпця — CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Qty])). Оскільки є row context, виклик CALCULATE ініціює Context Transition:
   - Значення Products[ProductKey] = 1 перетворюється в еквівалентний фільтр на таблиці Sales: Sales[ProductKey] = 1 (через існуюче відношення між таблицями).
3. Після переходу context, CALCULATE виконує SUM(Sales[Qty]) у новому filter context (тобто лише рядки Sales де ProductKey = 1): сумує Qty → 2 + 3 = 5.
4. Повертається 5 і записується в ProductTotalQty для рядка ProductKey=1.
5. Повтор для ProductKey=2: row context Products[ProductKey]=2 → Context Transition → SUM по Sales де ProductKey=2 → 4.

Ключова деталь: сам факт row context не робить SUM фільтрованим; саме CALCULATE (або CALCULATETABLE) робить перехід row→filter.

---

## Критичний робочий кейс: SUMX / ітератор з CALCULATE

Розглянемо сценарій, де у нас є таблиця Products і ми хочемо мірною (або обчислюваним стовпцем) пройти по кожному продукту і зібрати суму продажів виключаючи поточний продукт (наприклад, для обчислення "решта продажів"). Це ілюструє важливість правильного розуміння Context Transition.

Міра, що обчислює суму продажів по всіх продуктах крім поточного (в контексті перебору через SUMX):

```DAX
Measure_Others =
SUMX(
    Products,
    VAR CurrentPK = Products[ProductKey]
    RETURN
        CALCULATE(
            SUM(Sales[Qty]),
            ALL(Products),
            Products[ProductKey] <> CurrentPK
        )
)
```

Пояснення механіки:
- SUMX ітерує Products — для кожного рядка Products створюється row context, де Products[ProductKey] = поточне значення.
- Всередині SUMX викликається CALCULATE — це запускає context transition: row context → фільтр Products[ProductKey] = CurrentPK. Потім додатково застосовуються модифікації фільтра (ALL(Products) скидає існуючі фільтри по Products, а Products[ProductKey] <> CurrentPK додає фільтр, що виключає поточний продукт). Результатом є сума Sales[Qty] для всіх продуктів окрім поточного.

Якщо б ми не розуміли перехід контексту, помилково можна було б очікувати, що Products[ProductKey] <> CurrentPK просто прирівнюється локально, але без CALCULATE row context не трансформується, і фільтрація працюватиме не так, як очікується.

---

## Поширені помилки — чекліст для налагодження

- "Чому мій обчислюваний стовпець повертає лише значення поточного рядка замість сум по зв'язаній таблиці?"
  - Перевірте: чи використовуєте CALCULATE для ініціації Context Transition. Для обчислюваних стовпців агрегати над іншими таблицями зазвичай потребують CALCULATE або RELATEDTABLE.

- "Чому міра дає інший результат, ніж обчислюваний стовпець?"
  - Міра працює у filter context (контекст візуалу), а обчислюваний стовпець — в row context. Різниця очікувана; перевірте, де має сенс застосувати міру, а де — обчислюваний стовпець.

- "Я не розумію, коли CALCULATE робить transition і коли ні"
  - Правило: CALCULATE (і CALCULATETABLE) виконує Context Transition, якщо викликається в середовищі з row context. Якщо вже є тільки filter context (наприклад, при обчисленні міри у візуалі) — CALCULATE змінює filter context, але немає row→filter трансформації.

- "Чому SUMX(... SUM(...)) дає дивні результати?"
  - Пам'ятайте: SUMX створює row context для рядка, а внутрішній SUM потребує фільтрів — якщо ви хочете, щоб SUM враховував поточний рядок як фільтр, всередині SUMX слід використовувати CALCULATE(SUM(...)) щоб викликати Context Transition.

- EARLIER vs VAR: використовуйте VAR замість EARLIER там, де можливо; VAR робить логіку читабельнішою. Але це окрема тема — EARLIER/EARLIEST пов'язані з мульти-рівневим row context.

---

## Найкращі практики

- Використовуйте міри для агрегатів, коли можливе їх динамічне використання у візуалах — міри правильно реагують на фільтровий контекст від візуалів.
- Використовуйте обчислювані стовпці лише якщо потрібен матеріалізований результат на рівні рядка (наприклад, для категоризації або індексів). Для підсумків по зв'язаних таблицях в обчислюваному стовпці завжди застосовуйте CALCULATE.
- У ітераторах (SUMX, AVERAGEX, ADDCOLUMNS) свідомо застосовуйте CALCULATE у внутрішніх виразах, якщо ви хочете, щоб значення поточного рядка впливало як фільтр на інші таблиці.
- Тестуйте невеликими інваріантними наборами даних (як у цьому прикладі) щоб розуміти, як працює перехід контексту у кожному конкретному випадку.
- Документуйте де ви використовуєте CALCULATE з очікуванням Context Transition — це допомагає майбутнім розробникам зрозуміти логіку.

---

## Посилання / література

- "Context in DAX Formulas" — Microsoft Support (огляд понять row context та filter context). https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/context-in-dax-formulas-2728fae0-8309-45b6-9d32-1d600440a7ad
- "CALCULATE" — довідник DAX (функція, поведінка та приклади). https://dax.guide/calculate/
- "Context transition in DAX explained visually" — SQLBI article (глибока візуальна демонстрація механіки Context Transition). https://www.sqlbi.com/articles/context-transition-in-dax-explained-visually/
- Розділи щодо Context Transition у книзі "The Definitive Guide to DAX" — Marco Russo & Alberto Ferrari (SQLBI).

---

## Висновок

Context Transition — фундаментальна концепція DAX, яка пояснює, як row context може впливати на агрегати через перетворення у filter context за допомогою CALCULATE/CALCULATETABLE. Розуміння цього механізму необхідне для написання коректних обчислювань як у обчислюваних стовпцях, так і у мірах, а також для побудови надійних тестів і налагодження DAX-логіки.
